name: Definite & Indefinite Articles
summary: Master el, la, los, las (the) and un, una (a/an) — the building blocks of every Spanish noun phrase.
explanation:
Every noun in Spanish has a gender (masculine or feminine) and number (singular or plural). Articles must match both. Definite articles (el, la, los, las) translate to 'the' and refer to specific things. Indefinite articles (un, una, unos, unas) translate to 'a/an/some' and refer to non-specific things.

Unlike English, Spanish uses definite articles with general concepts: 'Me gusta el chocolate' (I like chocolate — all chocolate in general). This is one of the most common mistakes English speakers make. Spanish also uses articles with titles ('el señor García'), days of the week ('el lunes' = on Monday), and languages after certain verbs.

The neuter article 'lo' exists but isn't used with nouns — it's used with adjectives to create abstract concepts: 'lo bueno' (the good thing), 'lo importante' (what's important). This is a more advanced usage you'll encounter as you progress.

examples.es: Los niños juegan en el parque
examples.en: The children play in the park

examples.es: Tengo un gato y una perra
examples.en: I have a (male) cat and a (female) dog
I like coffee (in general — article required)

commonMistakes: Dropping the article with general concepts: 'Me gusta café' should be 'Me gusta el café'
Wrong gender: 'la libro' should be 'el libro' — libro is masculine
Using 'un' before feminine nouns starting with stressed 'a': it's 'el agua' but 'las aguas' — the noun is still feminine
Adding articles before professions with ser: 'Soy un profesor' should usually be 'Soy profesor'
tip: When in doubt about gender, remember: most nouns ending in -o are masculine, most ending in -a are feminine. But watch out for exceptions like 'el día' (masculine) and 'la mano' (feminine).
